data.title: Nasdaq plunges 3%, S&P drops 2%, and Dow falls as well as Wall Street sell-off continues
data.description:
Investors and traders on Wall Street have watched the broader market averages plunge on Monday with the Nasdaq Composite leading the way lower as tech sells-off. Concerns around the health of the economy continue to cast a dark cloud on equities. 

Falling the hardest has been the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) which has dropped 3.3% so far on Monday. Also deep in the red is the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) which is lower by 2% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) which has come down by 1%.

From a sector-by-sector stance, market participants observed that seven of the 11 S&P sectors trade lower with Info Tech and Communication Services suffering the most. At the other end of the spectrum Energy leads all other segments higher even as oil (CL1:COM) trades lower by 1.1%.

In the Treasury market, yields have declined as investors purchase bonds. The shorter end U.S. 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) fell by 6 basis points to 3.93%. Meanwhile, the longer end U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped 7 basis points to 4.23%.

The fears of a potential recession in the U.S. grew after Trump was asked if he was expecting a recession this year in the interview. “I hate to predict things like that,” he said. “There is a period of transition because what we’re doing is very big. We’re bringing wealth back to America. That’s a big thing.”

"Trump also failed to rule out a recession. After the 2024 soft landing, markets are getting more concerned about economic growth risks. As the pandemic illustrated, supply chain disruption can create growth problems. Disruption to trade and government both raise risks," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Furthermore, adding to the bearish sentiment, China’s return to deflation has fueled worries about global growth prospects.

China's consumer prices fell 0.7% year-on-year in February, a 13-month low, exceeding the expected 0.5% drop and reversing January’s 0.5% rise.

The economic calendar is light on Monday, with no real announcements being made. Investors will however receive the latest inflation data on Wednesday as markets get set to receive CPI data. 

U.S. consumers pushed up their short-term inflation expectations slightly and became more pessimistic about their future financial situations, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's February 2025 Survey of Consumer Expectations released on Monday.

As for stocks that are on the move, Moderna (MRNA) has climbed 4.1% despite Wall Street’s sell-off, while shares of MicroStrategy (MSTR) have dropped by 12.3%.

data.title: Viking, other obesity players fall following Novo Nordisk CagriSema results
data.description:
• Viking Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VKTX), Altimmune (NASDAQ:ALT), and Structure Therapeutics (NASDAQ:GPCR) are among the pharmaceutical companies developing weight-loss treatments that are also in the red following disappointing results released earlier Monday on Novo Nordisk's (NVO) next-gen obesity drug CagriSema.
  • Structure is taking the worst hit and is down ~8% in midday trading. It is developing an oral GLP-1R agonist, GSBR-1290.
  • Altimmune, which is down ~3%, has pemvidutide, a GLP-1/glucagon dual receptor agonist, set to enter phase 3. 
  • Viking, which initiated a phase 2 trial of an oral version of its GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist candidate VK2735 in January, is off ~1.5%.
  • Zealand Pharma (OTCPK:ZLDPF) and Veru (VERU), which are also developing obesity therapies, are down as well.

data.title: BCA Research: Expect a recession within the next 3 months with U.S. economy stalling
data.description:
The U.S. will enter a recession within a few months, according to BCA Research.

Strategists at BCA Research raised their 12-month U.S. recession probability to 75%, up from 65%, beginning “most likely” within the next three months.

Strategists have cut their recommended allocation to U.S. equities and are now underweight stocks. They are also overweight cash and recommend investors to increase fixed-income duration exposure.

“Conventional estimates understate the likely impact on economic activity from the trade war and DOGE cuts,” said Peter Berezin, chief global strategist at BCA Research. “This implies that growth will slow more than expected.”

He wrote that the insulation that household excess savings had in the economy a few years ago has worn out, with credit card, auto loan, and commercial real estate delinquencies on the rise.

In addition, the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model estimates that GDP is expected to decline by 2.4% in the first quarter.

“The full impact of the double-shock of the trade war and DOGE cuts yet to come, it is highly likely that growth will slow even more,” said Berezin. “This will translate into falling payrolls and rising unemployment during the second quarter this year.”

Conventional estimates underestimate the likely growth hit to the U.S. from higher tariffs, he said. “Most estimates ignore the supply-side effects of higher tariffs, focusing instead on the demand-side implications of rising import prices on real incomes.”

This could affect the auto industry, whose supply chain spans Mexico, Canada, and the U.S. It could also affect semiconductors, since 80% of those manufactured in the U.S. undergo advanced testing and assembly in Quebec, Canada, he said.

Tariffs will also lift inflation, making the Fed less likely to cut rates, even if temporarily.

Back in 2018 with the first Trump trade war, the PCE deflator was 6% below where it would have been if inflation had averaged 2% since 2008, Berezin said. Today, the PCE deflator is 1.2% above its trendline. “This means that the Fed has less scope to permit another inflation overshoot, especially if survey-based inflation expectations rise further.”

Given this, strategists have cut their recommended allocation to equities (DJI), (SP500), (COMP:IND), with the U.S. dollar (DXY) expected to continue weakening over the next few months.

More on U.S. economy:

  • Has the government forgotten what a real recession is?
  • US economy grows slightly in recent weeks: Fed's Beige Book report
  • US economy on path to 2% inflation, but policy should stay where it is, Atlanta Fed's Bostic says
  • US to launch probe into impact of Chinese chips, policies on American economy
  • Nasdaq drops 2%, S&P, and Dow fall as well as concerns about the economy grow

data.title: Methanex plunges as Geismar plant hit by unplanned outage, taking G3 offline; Scotiabank cuts
data.description:
Methanex (NASDAQ:MEOH) -12.3% in Monday's trading after saying its 1.8M metric ton Geismar 3 methanol plant in Louisiana was affected by an unplanned outage in late February, and the company now expected a plant startup by early May 2025.

The outage is expected to primarily impact Q2 financial results due to lower produced methanol sales, but the cost of related repairs is not expected to be material, the company said.

During the outage, Methanex (NASDAQ:MEOH) said it will take the opportunity to bring forward the completion of other planned maintenance work on G3 which was part of a previously budgeted three-week outage which is no longer expected to be required.

After the announcement, Scotiabank downgrades Methanex (MEOH) to Sector Perform from Sector Outperform with a $53 price target, cut from $66, saying that rightly or wrongly, yet another G3 issue will shake near-term investor confidence.

Analyst Ben Isaacson also believes macro headwinds are accelerating and markets are becoming increasingly shaky, adding that market meltdowns on growing recession risk would be most painful for deep cyclicals like Methanex (MEOH), but investors should add to any share price weakness that follows enactment of reciprocal tariffs starting April 2.

data.title: UDR downgraded at Truist Securities on below-average growth forecasts
data.description:
Multi-family residential REIT UDR (NYSE:UDR) was downgraded at the financial services firm Truist Securities on "slightly below-average three- and five-year growth" forecasts.

"Relatively high Metro Washington DC exposure (15%) might also become a headwind if job losses mount," said analyst Michael Lewis.

"The BLS reported U.S. nonfarm employment rose by 151K in February, but MSA level data for January will not be available until 3/21 and Challenger, Gray & Christmas reported 172K planned job cuts last month (62K announced by the federal government), the most since July 2020 during the first year of the COVID-19 pandemic," said the research note.

"Essentially, supply concerns should start to ease in many multifamily markets, just as some job/demand concerns might emerge," added the note.

"We continue to see the most value in CPT (Camden Property Trust) within our nine-company apartment REIT coverage universe," said the note.

Truist downgraded the stock to Hold, citing the stock has appreciated 17% over the last 12 months.

The rating aligns with Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating of Hold. Quant grades UDR as D for Valuation, C+ for Profitability, and D+ for Revisions.

Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors rate UDR as Buy.

data.title: TPT Global Tech postpones reverse stock split
data.description:
• TPT Global Tech (OTCPK:TPTW) said on Monday that it has decided to postpone its previously announced reverse stock split.
  • This decision reflects the company's ongoing assessment of market conditions, the company said.

data.title: Chip stocks sink amid fears of tariffs, economic growth woes
data.description:
Chip stocks fell on Monday amid fears of a potential downturn in the U.S. economy in light of recent tariff decisions, even as the Trump administration looked to deflect these concerns.

Shares of AI chipmakers, Nvidia (NVDA) fell about -5%, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) dipped around -3%.

Broadcom (AVGO) stock declined about -7%, while Marvell Technology (MRVL) tumbled nearly -8%. Intel (INTC) declined about -3%.

On Sunday, U.S. President Donald Trump brushed off concerns of business over his planned tariffs against many of the country’s trading partners, as well as the possibility of higher prices and a recession.

However, Bond traders have been preparing for a potential downturn in the U.S. economy amid the recent tariff decisions. They appear to be more pessimistic and are increasingly buying short-dated Treasuries, with the two-year yield declining sharply over the last two weeks.

Foundry leader Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) fell around -4%, while Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) dipped -3%, Analog Devices (ADI) -4%, and Texas Instruments (TXN) was down roughly -1%.

Chip equipment makers, ASML (ASML) slumped around -6%, Lam Research (LRCX) fell -5%, KLA Corp. (KLAC) -4% and Applied Materials (AMAT) -2%. 

GlobalFoundries (GFS) seemed to buck the trend by rising about +1% on Monday. Super Micro Computer (SMCI) also rose nearly +1%.

Meanwhile, Citi recently reaffirmed its bullish outlook on the broader market, even as Wall Street has experienced declines in recent weeks. The global investment firm is sticking to its year-end 2025 target of 6,500 for the S&P 500.

data.title: Urogen pharma outlines $1B revenue potential for UGN-102 with June 2025 launch target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UroGen Pharma (URGN) Q4 2024

Management View

  • Liz Barrett, President and CEO, highlighted the submission of a new drug application for UGN-102 and its ongoing FDA review with a PDUFA target date set for June 13, 2025. Barrett emphasized the potential for UGN-102 to address a significant unmet need in treating intermediate risk, low-grade, non-muscle invasive bladder cancer, projecting over $1 billion in revenue potential.

  • The company reported advancements in its pipeline, including updated 18-month duration of response data for the ENVISION Phase 3 trial. The study showed an 80.6% Kaplan-Meier analysis duration of response among patients achieving a complete response at the three-month mark. These findings were part of the NDA submission.

  • JELMYTO, the company's existing therapeutic product, generated $90.4 million in net product revenue for 2024, reflecting a year-over-year increase driven by higher underlying demand.

  • CFO Chris Degnan stated, "UroGen ended 2024 with $241.7 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities," and added that the company is "well-capitalized to execute its operating plan and advance to profitability."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed guidance for JELMYTO revenues in the range of $94 million to $98 million for 2025, marking an expected 8%-12% growth over 2024 demand-driven revenues.

  • The company anticipates higher operating expenses of $215 million to $225 million in 2025, primarily for expanded sales force and pre-commercial activities for UGN-102.

  • UroGen plans to increase its sales team from 52 to 83 representatives ahead of the UGN-102 launch, aiming to maximize market reach and adoption.

Financial Results

  • JELMYTO revenues for Q4 2024 were $24.6 million, slightly above the $23.5 million in Q4 2023. Full-year revenues reached $90.4 million compared to $82.7 million in 2023.

  • Net loss for Q4 2024 was $37.5 million or $0.80 per share, compared to a net loss of $26 million or $0.72 per share in Q4 2023. Annual net loss stood at $126.9 million compared to $102.2 million in the prior year.

  • Research and development expenses rose to $14.9 million in Q4 2024, driven by expenses tied to UGN-102 regulatory costs and UGN-103 trial activities.

Q&A

  • Michael Schmidt, Guggenheim, asked about launch trajectory expectations for UGN-102. David Lin, Chief Commercial Officer, responded that the shape of the launch would align with JELMYTO’s pattern but with higher absolute numbers due to a larger patient population.

  • Raghuram Selvaraju, H.C. Wainwright, inquired about reimbursement differences during the initial launch phase under a miscellaneous J-code. Liz Barrett explained that uptake would initially favor hospitals over private practices due to reimbursement risks, with smoother integration expected once a permanent J-code is secured in January 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in the potential market impact of UGN-102 and sought clarity on reimbursement scenarios. Their tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on data and commercialization logistics.

  • Management appeared confident and well-prepared for the UGN-102 launch, emphasizing robust pre-commercial activities, scalability, and physician engagement.

  • Compared to the prior quarter, management's tone reflected similar optimism but with heightened focus on execution and operational readiness for 2025.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • FDA’s acceptance of the UGN-102 NDA and progression to mid-cycle review is a major development since Q3 2024.

  • Year-over-year growth in JELMYTO revenue showed continued strong demand, while Q4 saw increased R&D expenses primarily for regulatory and trial activities.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from regulatory clarity in Q3 to commercialization strategies and launch preparedness in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged reimbursement challenges with the miscellaneous J-code for UGN-102 during its initial launch phase, emphasizing robust support strategies to mitigate these risks.

  • Analysts highlighted potential hurdles related to FDA review timelines and ODAC outcomes.

Final Takeaway

UroGen Pharma is positioned for transformative growth in 2025, driven by the potential approval and launch of UGN-102. The company projects over $1 billion in annual revenue for UGN-102, supported by strong clinical trial data and a comprehensive commercialization strategy. With ongoing advancements in its pipeline and a robust financial position, UroGen aims to establish itself as a leader in uro-oncology therapeutics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Net power outlines $1.7B-$2B cost target for SN1 clean gas power plant amid optimization efforts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NET Power Inc. (NPWR) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Danny Rice highlighted significant progress for 2024, including completing the front-end engineering and design (FEED) for Project Permian (SN1), the world's first utility-scale clean gas power plant. The company also initiated its equipment validation program with Baker Hughes at the La Porte facility, achieving over 140 fired hours during testing.
  • Management discussed inflationary pressures impacting cost estimates, revising the total installed cost for SN1 to $1.7 billion to $2 billion. The company is now focusing on post-FEED optimization to reduce costs while maintaining performance.
  • CEO Rice emphasized the strategic position of Net Power to address growing energy demand, particularly from AI and data centers. He stated that "Net Power can be the most logical solution to deliver clean, reliable and affordable energy."
  • CFO Akash Patel reported the company ended 2024 with $533 million in liquidity. He detailed operational cash outflows of $13 million and capital expenditures of $29 million for Q4, primarily for La Porte upgrades and SN1 development.

Outlook

  • Management outlined priorities for 2025, which include completing the value engineering process for SN1, advancing feasibility studies for Gulf Coast multi-unit projects, and securing capital for commercialization efforts.
  • CEO Rice stated that groundbreaking for SN1 is targeted for 2027, with an in-service date in 2029, contingent on securing necessary funding.
  • The company plans to focus on modular multi-unit deployments along the Gulf Coast to enhance scalability and cost efficiency. Management also signaled interest in pursuing industrial-scale applications through a collaboration with Baker Hughes and Woodside Energy.

Financial Results

  • CFO Patel highlighted operating cash outflows of $32 million and capital expenditures of $70 million for 2024. The company allocated $200 million of its liquidity for SN1 and has spent $50 million to date on engineering and long-lead items.
  • Patel noted that current SN1 economics support approximately $600 million in project-level financing, leaving a funding gap of $600 million to $900 million to fully fund the project.
  • Management is exploring multiple avenues to address the funding gap, including project-level capital, strategic partnerships, and potential government support.

Q&A

  • Analyst Thomas Meric, Janney Montgomery Scott: Questioned the breakdown of labor and material costs in the FEED estimate. COO Brian Allen explained that cost increases stem from market supply-demand imbalances, first-of-a-kind project specificities, and site-specific challenges like inland transport and nitrogen content in natural gas.
  • Analyst Martin Malloy, Johnson Rice: Inquired about modularization milestones and timelines. COO Allen confirmed work is underway to maximize modularization for coastal locations and reduce costs through pre-fabrication and multi-unit configurations.
  • Analyst Noel Parks, Tuohy Brothers: Raised concerns about power demand pressures driven by AI. CEO Rice noted that tight supply chains and rising grid demand reinforce the need for scalable, reliable solutions like Net Power's technology.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the scalability and funding of SN1 amid rising costs, reflecting a slightly negative sentiment.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing the long-term potential of their technology. However, responses to analyst questions were more defensive, particularly regarding cost overruns and funding gaps.
  • Compared to Q3, management's tone shifted to acknowledge challenges while reiterating their strategic focus on cost optimization and market positioning.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for SN1 costs increased from $1.1 billion to a range of $1.7 billion to $2 billion due to inflationary pressures and site-specific challenges.
  • In Q3, management emphasized technical progress at La Porte and early-stage project development. In Q4, the focus shifted to funding challenges and cost optimization efforts.
  • Q&A discussions revealed heightened analyst scrutiny on cost escalations and funding paths compared to the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Inflationary pressures and supply chain constraints remain significant challenges, impacting SN1 cost estimates and project timelines.
  • The company faces a funding gap for SN1 of $600 million to $900 million, with uncertainties around securing additional capital.
  • Project-specific challenges, such as inland transport and nitrogen purification, contribute to higher costs and complexity.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the scalability and competitiveness of Net Power's technology amid rising costs.

Final Takeaway

Net Power is advancing its innovative clean gas power technology but faces significant cost and funding challenges. The company remains focused on cost optimization, strategic partnerships, and modular deployment strategies to address escalating costs and market demand for reliable, clean energy solutions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Johnson and Johnson will not exercise worldwide license option for HexaBody-CD38, says Genmab
data.description:
Genmab A/S (Nasdaq: GMAB) said on Monday that Johnson & Johnson (J&J) has decided not to exercise its option to obtain a worldwide license for the development, manufacturing, and commercialization of HexaBody-CD38.

Despite initial clinical data showing efficacy, Genmab has decided to discontinue further clinical development of HexaBody-CD38.

“While we are disappointed that J&J has decided not to advance HexaBody-CD38, the data confirms the clinical potential of the HexaBody platform, reinforcing its value for future applications,” said Jan van de Winkel, Ph.D., Chief Executive Officer of Genmab.

Preliminary trial results submitted to J&J included data which demonstrated an overall response rate of 55%. However, due to the short follow-up period, key secondary measures such as duration of response, progression-free survival, and overall survival were not yet fully assessed.

Genmab  confirmed that this development will not impact its 2025 financial guidance.

GMAB shares are down about 7%.
